sections.title: Derivative Markets and Instruments - Basics
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: A derivative is a financial instrument whose VALUE DEPENDS ON (derives from) the value of an underlying asset. The underlying can be stocks, bonds, commodities, currencies, or interest rates.

WHY THIS MATTERS FOR THE EXAM: Derivatives represents 5-8% of the CFA Level I exam (approximately 9-14 questions). Understanding derivatives is ESSENTIAL for risk management and investment strategies.

FOUR MAIN TYPES OF DERIVATIVES:

  1. Forwards:
    • PRIVATE contract between two parties
    • To buy/sell an asset at a FUTURE DATE for a price agreed TODAY
    • Customized terms (not standardized)
    • Traded OVER-THE-COUNTER (OTC), not on exchanges
    • EXAMPLE: A farmer agrees to sell 10,000 bushels of wheat to a bakery in 3 months at $5/bushel. Both lock in the price today.
    • RISK: Counterparty risk (one party might default)
  2. Futures:
    • Like forwards, but STANDARDIZED and exchange-traded
    • Traded on organized exchanges (CME, CBOT)
    • Daily settlement (gains/losses settled daily)
    • Lower counterparty risk (clearinghouse guarantees)
    • EXAMPLE: Crude oil futures contract on NYMEX. Standardized: 1,000 barrels, specific delivery date.
  3. Options:
    • Gives the RIGHT (but NOT obligation) to buy or sell
    • Call Option: Right to BUY the underlying asset
    • Put Option: Right to SELL the underlying asset
    • Strike Price: The price at which you can buy/sell
    • Expiration: The date when the option expires
    • EXAMPLE: You buy a call option on Stock XYZ with strike price $50, expiring in 3 months. If XYZ rises to $70, you can exercise and buy at $50 (immediate $20 profit). If XYZ falls to $40, you DON'T exercise (you only lose the option premium).
  4. Swaps:
    • Agreement to EXCHANGE cash flows over time
    • Most common: Interest rate swaps
    • EXAMPLE: Company A has a floating-rate loan but wants fixed payments. Company B has a fixed-rate loan but wants floating. They SWAP payments. Company A pays fixed, receives floating. Company B pays floating, receives fixed.

DERIVATIVE PURPOSES:

  • Hedging (Risk Management): Reducing risk. EXAMPLE: An airline buys oil futures to lock in fuel costs. If oil prices rise, the futures gain offsets higher fuel costs.
  • Speculation: Betting on price movements. EXAMPLE: You think gold will rise, so you buy gold futures. High leverage = high risk!
  • Arbitrage: Exploiting price differences. EXAMPLE: Same stock trades at $100 on NYSE and $100.50 on LSE. Buy on NYSE, sell on LSE, pocket the $0.50 difference (risk-free profit).

DERIVATIVE PAYOFFS - CALL OPTIONS:

Stock Price at ExpirationCall Option PayoffAction
Above strike priceStock Price - Strike PriceEXERCISE (profit)
Below strike priceZeroDON'T EXERCISE (loss = premium paid)

EXAMPLE: Call option with $50 strike, premium = $3.

  • Stock at $60: Payoff = $60 - $50 = $10. Profit = $10 - $3 = $7
  • Stock at $45: Payoff = $0 (don't exercise). Loss = $3 (premium)

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'FFOS' - Forwards, Futures, Options, Swaps (the four derivative types). Also remember 'Call = Right to Buy, Put = Right to Sell'. Think: You CALL something to you (buy), you PUT something away (sell).
keyPoints: Derivatives = 5-8% of exam (9-14 questions)
Derivative value DERIVES from underlying asset
Forwards = Private, customized, OTC contracts
Futures = Standardized, exchange-traded, daily settlement
Call Option = RIGHT TO BUY at strike price
Put Option = RIGHT TO SELL at strike price
Options: Right but NOT obligation (unlike forwards/futures)
Swaps = Exchange of cash flows over time
Three purposes: Hedging, Speculation, Arbitrage
Counterparty risk highest in forwards (private contracts)
Futures have clearinghouse guarantee (low counterparty risk)
Memory aid: 'FFOS' - Forwards, Futures, Options, Swaps

formulas.expression: Call Option Payoff = Max(0, Stock Price - Strike Price)
Put Option Payoff = Max(0, Strike Price - Stock Price)

practiceQuestions.question: A call option gives the holder the right to:
options: Sell the underlying asset at the strike price
Buy the underlying asset at the strike price
Receive dividends from the underlying asset
practiceQuestions.explanation: A CALL option gives the holder the RIGHT TO BUY the underlying asset at the strike price. A PUT option gives the right to SELL. Remember: You 'call' something to you (buy it), you 'put' something away (sell it). EXAM TIP: This is a BASIC but ESSENTIAL concept. Know the difference between calls and puts cold.

practiceQuestions.question: Which derivative instrument has the HIGHEST counterparty (default) risk?
practiceQuestions.explanation: FORWARD contracts have the HIGHEST counterparty risk because they are PRIVATE agreements between two parties with no guarantee. If one party defaults, the other loses. Futures and exchange-traded options have a CLEARINGHOUSE that guarantees performance, eliminating counterparty risk. EXAM TIP: OTC derivatives (forwards, swaps) = high counterparty risk. Exchange-traded derivatives (futures, options) = low counterparty risk.

practiceQuestions.question: You buy a call option with a $50 strike price for a $3 premium. At expiration, the stock is trading at $55. What is your profit?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Call option payoff = Stock Price - Strike Price = $55 - $50 = $5. But you PAID $3 premium, so Profit = $5 - $3 = $2. EXAM TIP: Remember to subtract the premium! The payoff is $5, but the PROFIT is only $2 because you paid $3 upfront.

sections.title: Option Valuation - Intrinsic and Time Value
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: An option's price (premium) has TWO COMPONENTS: Intrinsic Value (what it's worth if exercised NOW) and Time Value (what you pay for the POSSIBILITY of future gains).

WHY THIS MATTERS: Option valuation questions are COMMON on the exam. Understanding intrinsic vs. time value is FOUNDATIONAL.

OPTION PREMIUM = INTRINSIC VALUE + TIME VALUE

INTRINSIC VALUE:

The profit you would make if you exercised the option RIGHT NOW.

  • Call Option: Intrinsic Value = Max(0, Stock Price - Strike Price)
  • Put Option: Intrinsic Value = Max(0, Strike Price - Stock Price)

THREE STATES:

Call OptionPut Option
In-the-MoneyStock > StrikeStock < Strike
At-the-MoneyStock = StrikeStock = Strike
Out-of-the-MoneyStock < StrikeStock > Strike

EXAMPLE: Call option with $50 strike:

  • Stock at $55: In-the-money, Intrinsic Value = $5
  • Stock at $50: At-the-money, Intrinsic Value = $0
  • Stock at $45: Out-of-the-money, Intrinsic Value = $0

TIME VALUE:

The EXTRA amount investors pay for the POSSIBILITY that the option will become more valuable before expiration.

Time Value = Option Premium - Intrinsic Value

FACTORS AFFECTING TIME VALUE:

  • Time to Expiration: MORE time = HIGHER time value (more chance for favorable moves)
  • Volatility: HIGHER volatility = HIGHER time value (bigger potential moves)
  • Interest Rates: HIGHER rates = HIGHER call time value, LOWER put time value

TIME DECAY:

As expiration approaches, time value DECREASES. At expiration, time value = ZERO (only intrinsic value remains).

EXAMPLE: Call option premium = $7, Stock = $55, Strike = $50.

  • Intrinsic Value = $55 - $50 = $5
  • Time Value = $7 - $5 = $2

Investors pay $2 extra for the possibility that the stock rises further before expiration.

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'ITV' - Intrinsic + Time Value = Option Price. Also remember 'Time Value DECAYS' - as expiration approaches, time value falls to zero. Out-of-the-money options have ONLY time value (no intrinsic value).
keyPoints: Option Premium = Intrinsic Value + Time Value
Intrinsic Value = Profit if exercised immediately
Time Value = Premium - Intrinsic Value
In-the-Money Call: Stock Price > Strike Price
In-the-Money Put: Stock Price < Strike Price
Out-of-the-Money options have ZERO intrinsic value
More time to expiration = HIGHER time value
Higher volatility = HIGHER time value
Time value DECAYS as expiration approaches
At expiration, time value = ZERO
Memory aid: 'ITV' - Intrinsic + Time Value = Price

formulas.expression: Call Intrinsic Value = Max(0, S - X)
Put Intrinsic Value = Max(0, X - S)
Time Value = Option Premium - Intrinsic Value

practiceQuestions.question: A call option with a $40 strike price is trading when the stock is at $45. The option premium is $7. What is the time value?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Intrinsic Value = Stock Price - Strike Price = $45 - $40 = $5. Time Value = Premium - Intrinsic Value = $7 - $5 = $2. The option has $5 of intrinsic value (immediate exercise profit) and $2 of time value (possibility of further gains). EXAM TIP: Always calculate intrinsic value FIRST, then subtract from premium to get time value.

practiceQuestions.question: An out-of-the-money call option has:
options: Only intrinsic value, no time value
Only time value, no intrinsic value
practiceQuestions.explanation: Out-of-the-money options have ZERO intrinsic value (exercising would lose money). They have ONLY time value - investors are paying for the POSSIBILITY that the option becomes in-the-money before expiration. EXAM TIP: Out-of-the-money = No intrinsic value. At expiration, out-of-the-money options expire WORTHLESS.
